title: The Consumer and the Market: Pricing and the Mechanisms for

outline.text: Scarcity, utility, and value: their roles in pricing, their relationship to the consumer
The satisfaction of material wants: the behaviour of consumers

children.text: National consumption levels in the private sector: trends in expenditures for goods and services
Factors influencing consumers' tastes and spending
The protection of consumer interests

outline.text: Markets as an economic institution in a mixed economy

children.text: Markets classified by reference to competition and monopoly
Purely competitive markets as distinguished from markets of imperfect competition: monopoly, oligopoly, and monopolistic competition
Influences affecting the behaviour of sellers under various competitive conditions
The concept of workable competition
Government regulation of monopolistic practices
Major types of markets
Markets for primary commodities
Markets for manufactured goods
Markets for money and capital: the market for short-term loans, the securities market
The market for labour and services [see 533.C.1.]
The counterpart of the market under full-scale economic planning: markets under socialism
The historical development of markets: the market in economic theory, the relationship of the market to social welfare and politics
Markets in international trade [see 533. F.]
The function of the market in the establishment of equilibrium between supply and effective demand

outline.text: The price system in capitalist economies

children.text: The price system as a means of organizing economic activity: the determination of what is to be produced, how goods are to be produced, and who gets the product
Limitations on and failures of the price system: areas in which the price system does not function
Control of prices by business: price-fixing [see C.l.a., above]
Government-established price controls and subsidies: regulations concerning public utilities and bank interest rates [see 534.B.4.b., and 534.B.6.b.]
Economic relationships not susceptible to control by prices: "externalities," such as air pollution and highway congestion [see also 737.C.1.]
Imperfect knowledge on the part of buyers as to alternative uses of their buying power [see B.2., above]
